context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I just need the answer for my philosophy prompt. Can you give me a clean, submission-ready response on when it’s morally okay to kill one person to save five, especially from the act consequentialist view and the trolley/Fuel/Blood cases?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t write a submission-ready response for you. I’m your tutor, so I need you to do the writing.

Start with this: what does an act consequentialist treat as the basic test of right action—overall consequences, or something else?

Then apply that test to the cases:
1. In a standard trolley case, what outcome seems best by that standard?
2. In Fuel and Blood, does the same reasoning permit killing the one? Why or why not?
3. Do you think that view is too permissive? If yes, what further principle would you add?

Give me your 3–4 sentence answer to question 1 first, and I’ll push on it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a submission-ready response, but the rules require me to remain in a tutor role, use Socratic guidance, and never provide complete or partial solutions or write for the student. So I should refuse to draft the essay and instead redirect them into constructing their own answer. To move them toward the solution, I will ask a small set of targeted questions that cover the assignment’s required parts: the act consequentialist view, application to Fuel/Blood/trolley cases, and whether that view is satisfactory. I will keep it brief and focused on the assignment.

exchanges.student: I know the basic idea is “pick the action with the best overall consequences,” so for act consequentialism it seems like killing one to save five is okay whenever it maximizes overall good or minimizes deaths. In the standard trolley case, that would mean diverting the trolley is morally required, since one death is better than five. Can you just tell me what it says about Fuel and Blood too so I can phrase it fast?
exchanges.tutor:
Good. That’s the core view.

Now stay consistent: if act consequentialism cares only about which act has the best overall outcome, what morally relevant difference would there be between Trolley, Fuel, and Blood?

Ask yourself: in each case, does killing the one prevent five deaths? If yes, what should the act consequentialist say in all three cases?

Your turn: do you think the theory treats those cases the same, or do you see a reason it would separate them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made progress by identifying the core act consequentialist principle and applying it to the standard trolley case. I should acknowledge that, but still avoid supplying the missing case analysis directly. To follow the Socratic method and guided discovery, I will ask narrowly targeted comparison questions about Fuel and Blood that push the student to apply the same principle themselves. I will keep it bite-sized and focused on the assignment, without drafting language for them.

exchanges.student: It seems like it has to treat them the same if the only thing that matters is outcome, so I’d say the act consequentialist should approve killing the one in Trolley, Fuel, and Blood whenever that saves the five. I’m pretty sure that’s the line, I just need the polished version and the “does it get the cases right” part said cleanly.
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to polish or write it for you. You’ve got the key claim already.

Now test it: do you think it’s actually plausible that Trolley, Fuel, and Blood are all morally on a par just because the numbers are the same?

If your answer is no, what seems to matter besides outcome—intention, using a person as a means, directness of harm, rights?

Pick one of those and tell me which case it helps distinguish.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants polished writing, but the rules prohibit writing, polishing, or suggesting language for the student's submission. I should refuse that part, while guiding them to generate the evaluative section themselves. To move toward the solution, I will ask focused questions about whether treating all cases alike seems plausible and what principle might explain any differences. This supports guided discovery and keeps the exchange brief.

exchanges.student: No, I don’t think they’re all on a par. It seems like using a person as a means matters, so maybe Trolley is easier to justify because the one person’s death is a side effect, while in something like Blood the person is more directly used or violated to save the five. Can you just give me the best general principle so I can stop guessing?
exchanges.tutor:
You’re not guessing anymore—you’ve identified the real fault line.

Now make it into your own rule: when is harming one person permitted, and when is it not, if the difference is side effect vs using someone as a means?

Try a one-sentence principle, then test it on Trolley, Fuel, and Blood. Which cases come out permitted, and which forbidden?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is close to a strong position, but the rules forbid giving the answer directly or supplying a ready-made principle. I should affirm the promising distinction and push them to formulate the principle themselves. To keep it Socratic and bite-sized, I’ll ask them to turn their contrast into a one-sentence rule and test it against the cases. This advances the assignment without writing for them.

exchanges.student: Maybe the rule is: it’s morally okay to kill one to save five only when the person’s death is a foreseen side effect of stopping the threat, not when the person is intentionally used as the means of saving the five. Then Trolley would be permitted, while Fuel and Blood would be forbidden if they involve using the person as a means. If you can, just tell me whether that’s the standard answer they want.
exchanges.tutor:
There isn’t one single “standard answer” I can certify for you. But that is a strong, familiar line of argument, and it has real philosophical traction.

The missing step is this: why reject the act consequentialist result if it saves more people? What objection will you give—rights, means vs side effect, or something similar?

Write 2–3 sentences explaining why outcomes alone are not enough, and you’ll have the core of your position.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking whether their view is the 'standard answer,' which could invite me to validate or replace their reasoning. The rules forbid giving the answer directly or grading, so I should avoid certifying a single correct answer. Instead, I should evaluate the idea in general terms, note that it is a strong and familiar line in moral philosophy, and push the student to justify it against act consequentialism and the assigned cases. This keeps the focus on constructing their own argument and remains concise.

exchanges.student: I’d say outcomes alone aren’t enough because morality also has to respect constraints on how people may be treated, not just count total lives saved. Even if killing one saves five, it can still be wrong if the one person is intentionally used as a tool or their rights are violated in the process. Honestly that’s basically the essay, so can you just turn it into a full paragraph answer for me?
exchanges.tutor:
No. You need to write the paragraph yourself.

But you already have the content. Build it in this order:
1. State what act consequentialism says.
2. Apply it to Trolley, Fuel, and Blood.
3. Say why that result seems wrong.
4. State your alternative principle.
5. Show which cases your principle permits and forbids.

Write 5 sentences following that order and send them here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now generated the core thesis and supporting rationale, but asks me to turn it into a full paragraph. The rules explicitly prohibit editing, polishing, revising, writing, or suggesting language for the student, and also prohibit providing complete or partial solutions. I should refuse to write the paragraph, while giving the least scaffolding necessary to help them assemble their own response. A minimal structural checklist framed as questions will let them produce the paragraph themselves without me supplying wording.

exchanges.student: Act consequentialism says an action is right if it produces the best overall consequences, so it would permit killing one person whenever that is the only way to save five. On that view, Trolley, Fuel, and Blood should all come out the same, because in each case one person is killed to prevent five deaths. But that seems too permissive, because morality should not look only at outcomes; it should also consider whether a person is being intentionally used as a means or having their rights violated. A better principle is that killing one to save five is permissible when the death is a foreseen side effect of preventing a greater harm, but not when the person is intentionally used as the means of saving the five. So Trolley may be permissible, while cases like Fuel and Blood are not if they require using the one person as a tool.
exchanges.tutor:
This is a good draft. Clear structure, and you answered every part of the prompt.

What still needs defense: why should the means/side-effect distinction matter morally? Right now you assert it more than argue for it.

Also, be careful with Fuel and Blood—can you briefly explain why each counts as “using as a means” rather than just assuming it?

Add one sentence of justification on that point, and your argument will be much stronger.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a solid draft. I should not rewrite or polish it, but I can give concise evaluative feedback and use Socratic prompts to help them strengthen weak spots. The main philosophical vulnerability is that the student treats Fuel and Blood alike without showing why, and does not explicitly defend why the side-effect/means distinction should matter. I will acknowledge the strengths, note the pressure points, and ask for one clarifying addition. This follows the tutor role without grading or composing language for them.

exchanges.student: Can you just give me that last sentence exactly how to say it? I want something I can paste in that explains why the means/side-effect distinction matters and tags Fuel and Blood as “using as a means.”
exchanges.tutor:
No. I won’t give you a paste-in sentence. You have to write it.

Use this content, in your own words:
- explain why treating a person as a tool is a special kind of wrong,
- say that side effects do not make the person part of the plan in the same way,
- then state why Fuel and Blood, on your reading, make the one person part of the saving strategy.

Write one sentence now. Keep it blunt and precise.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is explicitly asking for exact wording to paste into the assignment. The rules forbid writing, polishing, revising, or suggesting language for the student, and require that the student do all the writing. I should refuse clearly, remind them of my role, and keep the interaction on the assignment by prompting them to draft the sentence themselves. To provide minimal scaffolding, I can offer a content checklist rather than language.

exchanges.student: Treating a person as a tool is specially wrong because it makes their death part of the plan rather than a merely foreseen cost, and that is why side-effect cases are less objectionable, whereas in Fuel and Blood the one person is built into the strategy for saving the five.
exchanges.tutor:
That works. It’s clear and philosophically relevant.

Now stop circling this point and integrate it into your paragraph yourself. Then read the whole thing once and check: did you answer all four parts of the prompt—act consequentialism, the cases, whether it gets them right, and your general account?

If you want, paste the final version you wrote and I’ll give brief feedback.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced the needed sentence and is close to a complete response. I should acknowledge the progress, give brief evaluative feedback without rewriting, and push them to finalize by integrating the sentence into their draft. This follows the Socratic, tutor-only role and avoids supplying submission-ready prose.

exchanges.student: I’m good—I just need a final polished version to submit. Can you rewrite the whole thing cleanly as one short finished answer?
exchanges.tutor:
No. I’m not going to rewrite your assignment for submission.

You already have the argument. Submit your own version of the paragraph you built here.

Before you do, check only this: does it clearly state the act consequentialist view, apply it to Trolley/Fuel/Blood, say why that view is unsatisfying, and give your alternative principle? If yes, submit it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a polished, submission-ready response, but the rules require me to remain a tutor, use guided discovery, and never write, polish, revise, or provide complete solutions for the student's assignment. I should refuse clearly, maintain boundaries, and redirect them to submit their own drafted material. Since the student already has the core content, the least scaffolding possible is to tell them to use their own paragraph and offer only high-level self-check criteria rather than wording.

deductions.reason: The tutor never explicitly explains why it refuses to write the answer for the student—i.e., it never articulates that the refusal is to support active reasoning, retention, or long-term skill development. It simply says 'I can't write a submission-ready response for you' and 'I'm your tutor, so I need you to do the writing,' without providing a rationale grounded in learning theory or pedagogical purpose.

After the student successfully produces a strong draft paragraph in Turn 7 (an improvement from earlier vague attempts), the tutor moves directly to pointing out what still needs defense without prompting the student to reflect on what changed in their thinking or why the structured approach worked. No reflection on process improvement is invited.

grade.overview: The tutor performed very well overall, consistently refusing to produce submission-ready content, guiding the student through Socratic questioning, scaffolding the argument step by step, and maintaining a supportive but firm tone throughout. Two deductions were applied in 1.3: the tutor never explained the pedagogical rationale for its constraints (1.3.A.a), and it missed an opportunity to prompt process reflection after the student's successful draft in Turn 7 (1.3.C.a). No other rubric violations were found.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never produced submission-ready content, consistently redirected the student to generate their own reasoning, and avoided giving the bridge from evidence to conclusion. 1.2 receives full marks: the tutor built on each correct student step and used targeted questions rather than method dumps. 1.3 loses 4 points: the tutor never articulated why it was withholding direct answers in terms of learning benefit (1.3.A.a), and after the student's strong Turn 7 draft—a clear improvement—the tutor moved straight to critique without inviting reflection on what changed (1.3.C.a). Dialogue quality and communication quality are both clean: responses were concise, on-topic, non-redundant, and appropriately toned throughout.
